chunks.text: \chapter{Window Management with X~Windows or HP~VUE} \section{Introduction} \setcounter{mpfootnote}{0} \setcounter{footnote}{0} \renewcommand{\thempfootnote}{\arabic{mpfootnote}} On the DEC and HP workstations, you will want to run a window system an

kstations, you will want to run a window system and a window manager. A window manager operates on top of a windowing system. For example, {\it X Windows} is a window system. {\it Twm}, {\it mwm}, and {\it vuewm}\footnote{Vuewm is based on the Moti

nd {\it vuewm}\footnote{Vuewm is based on the Motif Window Manager and hence very similar.}. are window managers. {\it HP VUE} is the Hewlett Packard Visual User Environment, a system that includes an X11-based window manager and a window manager ca

n X11-based window manager and a window manager called {\it vuewm}. HP VUE and vuewm run only on the Hewlett Packard workstations, but X Windows runs on all the Instructional workstations. Please note that X Windows, HP VUE, twm, mwm, and vuewm are h

that X Windows, HP VUE, twm, mwm, and vuewm are highly customizable, so the set-up may not always remain the same through system upgrades, from one user account to another, etc. X Windows and HP VUE both let you divide your workstation's screen int

both let you divide your workstation's screen into one or more ``windows''. You enter information into a window by moving a ``mouse cursor'' into that window and typing on the keyboard. Each window acts like a separate terminal, and you can do dif

acts like a separate terminal, and you can do different things in each one. You can edit a file in one window, format a document in another, compile a program in another, view your mail messages in still another, and so on. X Windows and HP VUE bo

still another, and so on. X Windows and HP VUE both make the workstation a very powerful tool. After you create the windows you want to use, you can move them around and shuffle them above or beneath one another, making them any size you want. You

h one another, making them any size you want. You can also remove individual windows from your screen as you are done with them. You manipulate windows using the mouse, commands entered from your keyboard, or a combination of the two. \section{Of M

board, or a combination of the two. \section{Of Mice and Windows} The mouse\index{mouse} is an auxiliary input device that is attached to your workstation with a flexible cord. It is about the size of a hockey puck and has three buttons. You'll us

of a hockey puck and has three buttons. You'll use the mouse to control the mouse cursor\index{mouse!cursor} on your workstation screen. When the mouse cursor in inside most windows, it is shaped like a capital {\bf I} or an arrow, and when it is o

ke a capital {\bf I} or an arrow, and when it is over the background screen (the area outside any window), it is shaped like a capital {\bf X}. When the mouse cursor is over the background screen (also called the root window), typing on the keyboard

so called the root window), typing on the keyboard has no effect. You'll learn to perform three basic functions with the mouse: pointing, clicking, and dragging. Pointing\index{mouse!pointing} is simply moving the mouse to put the mouse cursor in t

mply moving the mouse to put the mouse cursor in the desired location. Clicking\index{mouse!clicking} is quickly pressing and immediately releasing one of the mouse buttons. Dragging\index{mouse!dragging} is holding down a mouse button, moving the m

ging} is holding down a mouse button, moving the mouse, then releasing the mouse button. \section{X Windows} \subsection{Opening Additional Windows} As you become more adept at using X Windows and other UNIX\/\TM software and services, you may find

ther UNIX\/\TM software and services, you may find it more convenient to work with multiple windows.\index{window!multiple} There are two easy ways to open additional windows. \subsubsection{Creating a Window Using the ``Root Menu''}\label{xtermW}

g a Window Using the ``Root Menu''}\label{xtermW} \index{window!creating} Move the mouse cursor onto the background screen (the cursor will change shape to resemble a capital X) and press the left mouse button. This displays the ``Root Menu''.\inde

use button. This displays the ``Root Menu''.\index{root menu} While holding down the left mouse button, move the mouse cursor (which is now shaped like an arrow pointing diagonally toward the upper right) downward until a box appears around the firs

ight) downward until a box appears around the first selection on the menu. Release the mouse button. Another window, labeled {\tt xterm } in the title bar\footnote{Other windowing systems often refer to the title bar as the banner.}, will appear fo

r to the title bar as the banner.}, will appear following a brief pause. The mouse will move to the new window after it opens. If you want this new window to be located in a different position on the screen, move the mouse until the mouse cursor is

screen, move the mouse until the mouse cursor is in the {\tt xterm } title bar (the rectangle containing the words {\tt xterm }), press down the left button, and while holding the button down, drag the window to a new location on the screen. \sub

he window to a new location on the screen. \subsubsection{Creating a Window with {\bf xterm}} \index{window!creating} \index{xterm@{\bf xterm} command} The {\bf xterm} program also lets you create an additional window on your screen into which yo

an additional window on your screen into which you can type commands and retrieve information. To create a new window using {\bf xterm}, enter at the prompt: {\tt \%} {\bf xterm \&} The system will respond with {\tt [1] xxx}, where {\tt xxx} is a

l respond with {\tt [1] xxx}, where {\tt xxx} is a number, followed by the {\tt \%} prompt. Another window will then open and the word {\tt xterm} will appear in the title bar of the newly opened window. If you get an error like the following: \beg

ow. If you get an error like the following: \begin{quote} {\bf Error: Can't open display:} \end{quote} make sure your DISPLAY environment variable is set properly. To set the DISPLAY, type: \begin{quote} {\tt \%} {\bf setenv DISPLAY hostname:0.0}

{quote} {\tt \%} {\bf setenv DISPLAY hostname:0.0} \end{quote} where hostname is the name of the machine you are using, for example, \begin{quote} {\tt \%} {\bf setenv DISPLAY lacerta:0.0} \end{quote} If you get a message saying ``Command not foun

e} If you get a message saying ``Command not found.'', make sure that you have /usr/bin/X11 and /usr/sww/X11/bin in your path. If you get a message like the following: \begin{quote} {\bf [1] + Stopped (tty output) xterm} \end{quote} Type ``{\bf fg

ed (tty output) xterm} \end{quote} Type ``{\bf fg}'' to bring the xterm process to the foreground and see more text about the error. You'll then need to fix whatever caused the error before starting up an xterm. Don't forget to enter {\tt \&} afte

up an xterm. Don't forget to enter {\tt \&} after {\tt term}. Using {\tt \&} causes the {\tt xterm} program to run in parallel with the first window as a concurrent process. If you don't type the {\tt \&}, you won't be able to work in your origin

{\tt \&}, you won't be able to work in your original window, because {\tt xterm} will be running in it. The {\tt [1]} means that this is your first background process and the {\tt xxx} is its Process Identification number (or PID). After you type {

Identification number (or PID). After you type {\tt xterm \&}, the mouse will move to the new window after it opens. If you want this new window to be located in a different position on the screen, move the mouse until the mouse cursor is in the

, move the mouse until the mouse cursor is in the {\tt xterm} bar (the rectangle containing the word {\tt xterm}), press down the left button, and while holding the button down, drag the window to a new location on the screen. If you open additional

ew location on the screen. If you open additional windows using {\bf xterm} versus using the ``Root Menu'', you may notice error messages as you close these windows. The following message will appear: {\tt xterm: fatal IO error 32 (Broken pipe)}

r: {\tt xterm: fatal IO error 32 (Broken pipe)} {\tt or KillClient on X server "}{\it nodename}{\tt .berkeley.edu:0.0"} where {\it nodename} is the name of your workstation. The {\tt \%} prompt will not reappear until you press \mykey{Return}. Yo

l not reappear until you press \mykey{Return}. You may avoid these messages by using the``Root Menu'' method of opening windows. \subsection{Customizing Your Windows}\index{window!customizing} You may customize any window by changing the size, pos

may customize any window by changing the size, position, font type, etc. To do this, just add some command line options to your xterm command, for example: \begin{quote} {\tt \%} {\bf xterm -T my\_title -rv } \end{quote} will create an xterm window

tle -rv } \end{quote} will create an xterm window with the title ``my\_title'' and in reverse video. Type {\bf man xterm} from the {\tt \%} prompt for more details. You can also put customizations in a .twmrc or .Xdefaults file in your home directo

n a .twmrc or .Xdefaults file in your home directory. This is a bit more complicated, and if you make any mistakes you may not be able to log back in to your account. You can customize just about everything. For example, here is my .Xdefaults file:

rything. For example, here is my .Xdefaults file: \begin{verbatim} XTerm*scrollBar: true XTerm*font: -adobe-courier-*-r-*-*-0-140-*-*-*-*-*-* #XTterm*Font: 9x15 XTterm*Geometry: 80x55 xterm*fontList: -ado

eometry: 80x55 xterm*fontList: -adobe-courier-*-r-*-*-0-140-*-*-*-*-*-* #ifdef COLOR xterm*background: azure1 #endif \end{verbatim} It is recommended that you get a book on X Windows if you intend to customize to this extent. \

dows if you intend to customize to this extent. \subsection{Getting Rid of a Window}\index{window!closing}\label{logout} To get rid of an {\tt xterm} window, move the mouse cursor into the window to be closed and type {\bf exit} then press \mykey{R

be closed and type {\bf exit} then press \mykey{Return}. This kills the {\tt xterm} process and makes the window disappear. You may also close a window by placing the mouse cursor in the small box in the upper left-hand corner and double clicking th

the upper left-hand corner and double clicking the left mouse button. You do not have to close all of the windows that you've created before you logout, although it is advisable to do so. Your final logout process will get rid of all open window

nal logout process will get rid of all open windows and the processes associated with those windows. Remember, shutting down all of your windows {\it does not always} log you off the machine. To logoff,\index{logging off} you must exit completely f

off,\index{logging off} you must exit completely from the machine. On the Sun and SGI workstations, hold down the right mouse button and select the last menu item. A good rule of thumb when logging off from an X Windows session is to make sure you s

ff from an X Windows session is to make sure you see the {\tt login:} prompt on the screen before you leave. That way you know you are logged off completely. \subsection{The Window Manager}\index{window manager} There are several ways to issue mos

ndow manager} There are several ways to issue most window manager commands.\index{window manager!commands} You can use the mouse in conjunction with the location of the mouse pointer on the screen; you can use the mouse in conjunction with the \myke

ou can use the mouse in conjunction with the \mykey{Meta}\index{metacharacter key (\key{META})} and \mykey{CTRL}\index{control key (\key{CTRL})} keys ({\tt Meta} is labeled {\tt Compose Character}\index{Compose Character key (\key{Compose Character})

ex{Compose Character key (\key{Compose Character})}, {\tt Esc}\index{Escape key (\key{Esc})}, or {\tt Alt}\index{Alt key (\key{Alt})} depending upon your terminal type); or you can choose commands from a pull-down menu. You issue commands from the m

m a pull-down menu. You issue commands from the mouse with various mouse button pointing, clicking and dragging combinations. Table~\ref{twm} summarizes the different ways to execute commands with {\bf twm} using the mouse and keys. \begin{table

\bf twm} using the mouse and keys. \begin{table}[hbt] \begin{center} \isucaption{Window Manager Commands} \label{twm} \def\arraystretch{1.5} \footnotesize \begin{tabular}{|l|l|} \hline \multicolumn{2}{|c|}{{\bf twm} Commands} \\ \hline \hfil Actio

{2}{|c|}{{\bf twm} Commands} \\ \hline \hfil Action \hfil & \hfil Method \\ \hline Move & Title Bar-{\myleft}-Drag \\ \hline Iconify & RightCorner-smallbox-{\myleft}-Click \\ \hline Deiconify & Icon-{\myleft}-Click \\ \hline Shuffle Up & Title Bar-{

{\myleft}-Click \\ \hline Shuffle Up & Title Bar-{\myleft}-Click \\ \hline Menu & Root Window$+$Any Mouse Button \\ \hline \end{tabular} \end{center} \normalsize \end{table} \subsubsection{Move a Window}\index{window!moving} When you execute this c

dow}\index{window!moving} When you execute this command and click on a window, that window will be outlined. While holding the mouse button down, move the mouse. Once you have your window located where you want it, release the mouse button and yo

where you want it, release the mouse button and your window will reappear in its new location. \subsubsection{Iconify a Window}\index{window!iconifying} When you iconify a window, you replace it with a small symbol that represents a window (an icon

h a small symbol that represents a window (an icon). The icon ``remembers'' the size and location of that window, so when you restore the iconified window, it will appear in its old location. You should iconify windows if there are too many windows f

ld iconify windows if there are too many windows for you to see easily. When you execute the iconify command, your active window will disappear and its icon will appear in its place. You can move icons the same way you move windows, so you can crea

ns the same way you move windows, so you can create your own method for organizing your workspace. \section{HP VUE}\index{HP VUE} \subsection{Login Options}\index{HP VUE!Options} Before you login to one of the Hewlett Packard workstations, you shou

one of the Hewlett Packard workstations, you should see a login banner with several buttons at the bottom of the screen. To get help with logging in, click the left mouse button on \key{Help}.\index{HP VUE!Help} Press and drag the left mouse button

HP VUE!Help} Press and drag the left mouse button on \key{Options} to see the menu of options. The following table shows the most useful options: \par \par \begin{minipage} {5.5in} { \begin{tabular}{l l l} \multicolumn{2}{c}{HP VUE Login Options}

}{l l l} \multicolumn{2}{c}{HP VUE Login Options} \\ \label{VUEoptions} \hfil Option \hfil & \hfil Purpose\\ \hline Restart server & This will restart the window system. This is useful \\ & if the login banner is messed up. \\ No Windows & Login w

ogin banner is messed up. \\ No Windows & Login with no window system at all. This is usually \\ & not necessary. \\ Fail-Safe session& Force a login session, even if there are errors in \\ & your .vueprofile, .vuewmrc or other startup files. \\ &

.vueprofile, .vuewmrc or other startup files. \\ & This option is very useful for debugging your startup files. \\ HP VUE Lite Session & Use a ``Lite'' version of HP VUE. This is similar \\ & to HP VUE, but has less functionality and is less of a dr

UE, but has less functionality and is less of a drain \\ & on the computer. \footnote{If you select HP VUE Lite, you will continue to get ``Lite'' every time you login unless you specifically change it back or don't run VUE at all.} \\ \end{tabular}

t back or don't run VUE at all.} \\ \end{tabular} } \end{minipage} \par \par \subsection{The Basics} Most of the information in the previous section on X Windows also applies to HP VUE, but there are some differences that will be covered here. Whe

e some differences that will be covered here. When you first login (assuming you haven't selected any different options from the \key{Options} menu), you should see one {\it hpterm} window (basically like an ``xterm'' window), a {\it file manager} w

like an ``xterm'' window), a {\it file manager} window and a {\it control panel} along the bottom of the screen. (You may be prompted for your terminal type in the hpterm window; if so, move the mouse cursor to that window, {\bf click the left mous

se cursor to that window, {\bf click the left mouse button,} and type ``hpterm''.)\index{hpterm} The file manager \index{HP VUE!file manager} is similar to the Apple Macintosh interface which shows an icon for each file or directory in your current

n icon for each file or directory in your current directory. The control panel \index{HP VUE!control panel} provides quick access to many applications and accessories. To select something from the control panel, click the icon with the left mouse

control panel, click the icon with the left mouse button. There will be documentation in the HP lab with more details about the items available on the control panel. It's best to just experiment with these tools by clicking on the icons. \subsectio

these tools by clicking on the icons. \subsection{Opening Additional Windows} To open up another window, click the left mouse button on the terminal icon at the lower left side of the control panel. If you aren't sure which icon this is, ask a neig

If you aren't sure which icon this is, ask a neighbor, or see the signs that should be posted in the lab. Also, see the information in Section \ref{xtermW}. Depending upon the configuration of your window manager, you may need to move the mouse po

window manager, you may need to move the mouse pointer to the particular window you would like to use and {\bf click the left mouse button} in order to use another window. \subsection{How to Logout from HP VUE}\label{logout2} To exit HP VUE, i.e.,

from HP VUE}\label{logout2} To exit HP VUE, i.e., logout from the HP workstation, click the left mouse button on the \key{Exit} icon on the lower right side of the control panel. You will then be prompted to confirm this by clicking on the \key{OK}

mpted to confirm this by clicking on the \key{OK} button. You can also move the mouse cursor onto the background screen (the cursor will change shape to resemble a capital X) and press the right mouse button. Select \key{Logout...} from the menu, t

e button. Select \key{Logout...} from the menu, then confirm this by clicking on the \key{OK} button. It's a good idea to always wait until you see the login banner reappear on the screen to be sure you are properly logged out. If the login banner

you are properly logged out. If the login banner does not reappear, you can hold down the three keys \key{Shift}~\key{Ctrl}~\key{Reset} at the same time. This will log you out and restart the window manager. If you don't logout properly, your accou

manager. If you don't logout properly, your account is not secure. \subsection{Starting X Windows instead of HP VUE}\label{novue} On the Hewlett Packard workstations, you will automatically get the HP Visual User Environment unless you specify othe

HP Visual User Environment unless you specify otherwise. Therefore, on the HP workstations only, if you want to use X Windows instead of HP VUE, create a file in your home directory called ``.no\_vue'' or ``.novue'': {\tt \%} {\bf cd;~~~touch .novue

' or ``.novue'': {\tt \%} {\bf cd;~~~touch .novue} then login as your normally would. If you change your mind and wish to go back to using HP VUE, just delete that file: {\tt \%} {\bf cd;~~~rm .novue} Please note that the above feature is unique

vue} Please note that the above feature is unique to the EECS Instructional systems. \subsection{Problems with HP VUE} If you have problems logging in to the HP workstation, select the \key{Options} button, then select \key{Restart Server}. This w

button, then select \key{Restart Server}. This will restart the HP VUE server. If the HP workstation is up, but there is no login banner, type \ctrl{d} a few times. The HP VUE server should then restart. If it doesn't, there may be another proble

estart. If it doesn't, there may be another problem. Please report this by sending e-mail to ``root''. If you aren't sure what's wrong with the display, type \key{Shift}~\key{Ctrl}~\key{Reset}. This will logout any active console processes and re

is will logout any active console processes and restart the HP VUE server. This will not affect any users who are logged in remotely. If you edited the {\tt .vueprofile} file in your home directory and are now unable to login, select the \key{Option
nd are now unable to login, select the \key{Options} button, then select \key{Fail-Safe Session}. This will allow you to login and fix any problems in your .vueprofile.
